terms.def: Internal Rate of Return — discount rate that makes NPV = 0; the standard PE return metric. Sensitive to timing of cash flows.
Multiple on Invested Capital — total cash returned / invested capital; simple but ignores time value of money.
Total Value to Paid-In = (NAV + distributions) / capital called; shows total value including unrealised.
PE manager's 20% profit share above the hurdle rate; the primary incentive alignment mechanism.
Request from the PE fund to investors for a portion of committed capital when an investment is made.
Distributions to Paid-In — realised return multiple; cash actually returned to investors / capital invested.

eli3: Private equity (PE) is investment in companies not listed on public stock exchanges. PE firms raise capital from institutional investors (pensions, endowments, sovereign wealth funds) into a fund, then use that capital — plus significant debt — to buy companies, improve them over 3-7 years, and sell them for a profit. The 'private' part means no public market pricing until exit.

Leverage is the engine of PE returns: a PE firm might put in $30M equity to buy a $100M company, borrowing the remaining $70M. If the company grows and sells for $150M, the equity return is not 50% but 400% (from $30M to $120M). This amplifies both gains and losses. Operational improvement and multiple expansion (selling at a higher valuation multiple than bought) are the other return drivers.

PE fees: '2 and 20' like hedge funds — 2% management fee on committed capital, 20% carried interest (carry) on profits above the preferred return (typically 8%). Lock-up periods are 7-12 years — capital is illiquid. The illiquidity premium: investors expect higher returns than public equity to compensate for not being able to sell.

whenWhy.title: PE return decomposition: leverage, operational improvement, multiple expansion
whenWhy.body: PE returns decompose into three sources: (1) Leverage effect: using debt to amplify equity returns. A company bought at 8× EBITDA with 70% debt (5.6× net debt/EBITDA): if EBITDA grows 20% and sold at same 8× multiple, the enterprise value grows 20%, but equity value grows proportionally more (debt is fixed). IRR contribution from leverage: approximately (entry_debt / entry_equity) × EBITDA growth rate; (2) Operational improvement: EBITDA margin expansion, revenue growth, cost reduction, strategic acquisitions. PE firms assign operational partners (former CEOs, CFOs) to portfolio companies. EBITDA growth contribution: (exit_EBITDA / entry_EBITDA - 1); (3) Multiple expansion: selling the company at a higher EV/EBITDA multiple than purchase. Occurs when: market conditions improve, company has better growth prospects, market re-rates the sector. Multiple expansion contribution: (exit_multiple - entry_multiple) / entry_multiple × entry_EBITDA / entry_EV. Historical decomposition: PE excess returns vs. public equity ~3-5%/year; attribution: ~50% operational, ~30% multiple expansion, ~20% leverage (varies by study).

whenWhy.title: LBO structure and mechanics
whenWhy.body: Leveraged Buyout (LBO): PE firm acquires a company primarily with debt. Typical LBO structure: Enterprise Value = 8-12× EBITDA; Debt = 4-7× EBITDA (senior secured + subordinated debt + mezzanine); Equity = 1.5-4× EBITDA (PE fund contribution + management rollover). Debt tranches: (1) Revolving credit facility: flexible draw/repay working capital facility; (2) Term Loan A/B: secured amortising senior debt (Term Loan B is bullet maturity, commonly 7-year); (3) Senior secured notes: high-yield bonds; (4) Mezzanine / PIK (Payment-in-Kind): subordinated, pays interest as additional debt (PIK toggle). LBO model key metrics: (a) DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio) = EBITDA / interest expense + amortisation. Must be > 1.0×; typically targeted at 1.5-2.5×; (b) Leverage ratio: Net Debt / EBITDA; covenants typically require < 6-7×; (c) IRR target: PE firms typically target 20-25% gross IRR, 15-20% net IRR to LPs; (d) MOIC (Multiple on Invested Capital): exit equity / invested equity. A 3× MOIC over 5 years ≈ 25% IRR.

whenWhy.title: Private equity fund performance metrics
whenWhy.body: Key performance metrics: (1) IRR (Internal Rate of Return): time-weighted return on cash flows. Sensitive to early distributions (cash returned quickly inflates IRR). PE funds target 15-25% net IRR; median is 12-15%. J-curve: PE funds show negative IRR in early years (fees paid, no exits) and improve as companies mature; (2) MOIC (Multiple on Invested Capital): total value to paid-in capital. MOIC = (Realized Value + Unrealized Value) / Total Capital Invested. MOIC of 2.5× means the fund returned 2.5× invested capital. Agnostic to time — a 2.5× over 10 years is worse than 2.5× over 5 years; (3) DPI (Distributions to Paid-In): how much has been returned in cash. DPI < 1× means no cash has been returned yet. DPI = 1× means investors got their money back; (4) RVPI (Residual Value to Paid-In): unrealised portfolio value / invested capital. Early-fund metric; (5) TVPI = DPI + RVPI. The J-curve dynamic: TVPI typically dips below 1× in years 1-3 (fees, early losses), then recovers and grows in years 4-8 as companies mature and exit.

deepDiveIntro: LBO IRR calculation: cash flows to equity = -equity_invested at t=0; +equity_proceeds at exit. IRR solves: 0 = -E₀ + Σ_t (dividends_t)/(1+IRR)^t + E_exit/(1+IRR)^T. For a simple case (no interim dividends, 5-year hold): E_exit = (EV_exit - Net_debt_exit). EV_exit = EBITDA_exit × exit_multiple. Net_debt_exit = initial_debt - EBITDA_FCF_conversion × EBITDA × years (debt repaid over hold period). IRR sensitivity: (a) Entry multiple: lower entry = higher IRR all else equal; (b) Exit multiple expansion: strong driver; (c) EBITDA growth: operational improvement; (d) Debt repayment: higher FCF conversion (EBITDA to cash) reduces exit debt, improves IRR; (e) Hold period: shorter hold amplifies IRR for same absolute return.

deepDive.heading: PE Fund Structures and LP/GP Dynamics
deepDive.body: Fund structure: Limited Partnership. General Partner (GP) = PE firm; manages investments, has unlimited liability in theory (protected by management company structure). Limited Partners (LPs) = institutional investors (pensions, endowments, family offices, sovereign wealth funds); contribute capital, have limited liability (lose only invested capital). Economics: GP: 2% management fee (on committed capital during investment period; on invested capital thereafter) + 20% carried interest (after preferred return of 8%/year). LP: committed capital deployed over 3-5 years; distributions from exits; 80% of profits above preferred return. Capital structure of a $1B fund: LP commit $1B over 5 years; GP invests (draws down commitments) over the investment period; exits occur 3-10 years later; distributions returned to LPs as companies are sold. Typical fund lifecycle: (1) Fundraising (0-1.5 years): GP raises capital from LPs; (2) Investment period (1.5-5 years): capital drawn down and invested; (3) Harvest period (5-12 years): portfolio companies mature, exit via IPO or sale; (4) Fund wind-down (10-14 years): last exits completed, distributions made. LPA (Limited Partnership Agreement): governs all terms; LP rights include: LPAC (LP Advisory Committee) for conflicts; key man clause (fund pauses if key GPs leave); no-fault divorce (LPs vote to remove GP). [1]

deepDive.heading: PE vs. Public Equity: Illiquidity Premium and Benchmark Comparison
deepDive.body: Do PE funds outperform public equity? The question is complex because: (1) Appraisal-based valuation: PE companies are valued by the GP at quarterly appraisals (not mark-to-market). This smooths reported returns and understates true volatility; (2) Comparison benchmark: what's the right public equity benchmark? S&P 500? Small-cap (PE targets tend to be smaller companies)? Leveraged small-cap (to match PE's debt level)? Public Market Equivalent (PME) method (Kaplan & Schoar 2005): invests PE cash flows into the S&P 500 index. PME > 1.0 = PE outperformed equivalent public investment. Studies: pre-2000 PE: PME ≈ 1.2-1.3 (clear outperformance); post-2000 PE: PME ≈ 1.0-1.1 (modest outperformance or in line after fees). Large PE funds (>$5B): PME ≈ 0.9-1.0 (underperforms at scale). Small/mid-market PE: PME ≈ 1.2-1.3 (better operational improvement opportunities). Illiquidity premium: compensation for 7-12 year lock-up. Theory: 2-4%/year premium over public equity. In practice: the premium has compressed as more capital flows into PE, driving up entry multiples. [2]

deepDive.heading: Secondary Markets and Co-Investments
deepDive.body: PE secondary markets: LPs can sell their fund interests before maturity in the secondary market. Buyers (secondary funds: Blackstone Secondary, Lexington Partners) purchase LP positions at discounts (10-30%) to NAV. Benefits for sellers: liquidity, portfolio rebalancing, cash needs. Benefits for buyers: J-curve mitigation (immediate exposure to mature portfolio), discount to NAV, known portfolio. Growth: PE secondaries market grew from $2B (2000) to $130B+ (2023); increased institutional acceptance. GP-led secondaries: GP restructures the fund, rolling remaining assets into a new vehicle. LPs can take liquidity or roll. Controversial: GP sets both buyer price and seller price. Co-investments: LPs investing directly alongside the PE fund in specific deals. No fee, no carry on co-investment amount (or reduced). Benefits: improved net return (avoid 2+20 on that capital), direct exposure to specific company. Drawbacks: selection bias (GPs offer co-investments on deals where they have capital constraints — sometimes the best deals, sometimes because deal is too large); requires internal investment team capable of underwriting. Large LPs (CPPIB, GIC, Abu Dhabi) build co-investment programmes to reduce fee drag while maintaining PE exposure. [1]

takeaways: PE return drivers: leverage amplification, EBITDA growth (operational), multiple expansion (market/rerating). [1]
Key metrics: IRR (time-weighted), MOIC (total return multiple), DPI (cash distributed), TVPI = DPI + RVPI. [1]
J-curve: negative early IRR due to fees and unrealised value; turns positive as companies mature and exit. [1]
PME benchmark: post-2000 PE shows PME ≈ 1.0-1.1; small/mid-market PE outperforms; large PE at scale underperforms. [2]
Co-investments: no fee/carry; improves net LP return; secondary market at 10-30% discount to NAV. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does leverage affect the IRR vs. MOIC trade-off in a PE investment?
edgeCases.a: The IRR/MOIC trade-off due to leverage: (1) High leverage amplifies equity returns (higher MOIC) but also increases the probability of losing all equity if the company underperforms. Example: $100M EV company, 80% debt ($80M), 20% equity ($20M). Exit scenarios: (a) EV exits at $120M: equity proceeds = $120M - $80M = $40M. MOIC = 2.0×, IRR depends on hold period (2.0× over 3yr = 26% IRR, over 5yr = 15% IRR); (b) EV exits at $90M: equity proceeds = $10M. MOIC = 0.5× (partial loss). (c) EV exits at $75M: company can't repay debt, equity = zero. MOIC = 0. (2) High leverage increases bankruptcy risk: if EBITDA falls during a recession and the company can't service debt, PE fund loses all equity. The 2008 PE debt crisis: many LBOs done at 7-9× EBITDA in 2006-2007 became distressed. (3) IRR vs. MOIC: PE firms sometimes structure deals with early dividends to boost IRR (getting capital back early) at the expense of MOIC (returning capital reduces the equity base that would compound). 'Dividend recapitalisation': portfolio company takes on new debt and pays dividend to PE fund. Inflates IRR metric but strips capital from the company. Controversial practice. Best practice: evaluate both IRR and MOIC together; MOIC is more robust to timing games.

edgeCases.q: What is EBITDA manipulation in PE and how does it affect valuations?
edgeCases.a: EBITDA manipulation in PE: companies (and their PE sponsors) often report 'Adjusted EBITDA' that includes add-backs — expenses subtracted from EBITDA to make the company appear more profitable. Common add-backs: (1) One-time restructuring costs (but restructuring happens every year); (2) Management fees paid to PE sponsor (legitimate but inflates base EBITDA); (3) Stock-based compensation (real cost, widely added back); (4) 'Run-rate' synergies (projected cost savings from future initiatives — not yet achieved); (5) COVID-19 adjustments (2020-2022: removing the bad year as 'non-recurring'); (6) Revenue growth adjustments: annualising recent monthly revenue as if it will persist. Impact: median PE company reported EBITDA add-backs ≈ 15-20% of reported EBITDA (Bernstein, 2024). A company with $100M GAAP EBITDA might report $120M adjusted EBITDA. If the company is purchased at 12× adjusted EBITDA = $1.44B, but actual GAAP EV/EBITDA = 14.4×. This inflates entry multiples and debt loads. Lender risk: loans are sized as multiple of adjusted EBITDA; if adjustments don't materialise, actual leverage is higher than underwritten. Regulatory response: SEC scrutiny of adjusted EBITDA in IPO filings; leveraged lending guidance from regulators limits acceptable adjustments. For investors: focus on FCF (Free Cash Flow) conversion — cash flow after capex is harder to manipulate than EBITDA.

sources.label: Kaplan, S.N. & Schoar, A. (2005) — Private Equity Performance: Returns, Persistence, and Capital, JF
Harris, R.S., Jenkinson, T. & Kaplan, S.N. (2014) — Private Equity Performance: What Do We Know?, JF
